news_summary: Scientists have made breakthroughs in understanding brain cells linked to depression, uncovered why some elderly maintain youthful memory, developed a gut reset to prevent weight gain post-medication, and created virus-destroying plastic, advancing health and wellness.
news_summary_fr: Les scientifiques ont réalisé des avancées décisives dans la compréhension des cellules cérébrales liées à la dépression, ont découvert pourquoi certaines personnes âgées conservent une mémoire jeune, ont mis au point une méthode de rééquilibrage intestinal pour prévenir la prise de poids après un traitement médicamenteux et ont créé un plastique capable de détruire les virus, contribuant ainsi à faire progresser la santé et le bien-être.
news_summary_es: Los científicos han logrado avances decisivos en la comprensión de las células cerebrales relacionadas con la depresión, han descubierto por qué algunas personas mayores conservan una memoria juvenil, han desarrollado un método para restablecer la flora intestinal con el fin de prevenir el aumento de peso tras la medicación y han creado un plástico capaz de destruir virus, lo que supone un gran avance para la salud y el bienestar.

articles.title: For the first time, scientists pinpoint the brain cells behind depression
articles.summary: Scientists have identified two specific types of brain cells that behave differently in people with depression, offering a clearer picture of what is happening inside the brain. By analyzing donated brain tissue with advanced genetic tools, the researchers found changes in neurons linked to mood and stress, as well as in immune-related microglia cells. These differences point to disruptions in key brain systems and reinforce that depression is rooted in biology, not just emotions.
articles.content:
Researchers at McGill University and the Douglas Institute have discovered that two distinct types of brain cells function differently in people with depression.

The findings, published in Nature Genetics, offer important clues that could lead to new treatments designed to target these specific cells. They also provide a clearer understanding of depression, a condition that affects more than 264 million people worldwide and remains a leading cause of disability.

"This is the first time we've been able to identify what specific brain cell types are affected in depression by mapping gene activity together with mechanisms that regulate the DNA code," said senior author Dr. Gustavo Turecki, a professor at McGill, clinician-scientist at the Douglas Institute and Canada Research Chair in Major Depressive Disorder and Suicide. "It gives us a much clearer picture of where disruptions are happening, and which cells are involved."

Rare Brain Tissue Enables Breakthrough

To make this discovery, the research team relied on post-mortem brain samples from the Douglas-Bell Canada Brain Bank. This collection is one of the few in the world that includes donated brain tissue from individuals who had psychiatric conditions, making it an invaluable resource for studying mental health at a biological level.

Using advanced single-cell genomic techniques, the scientists examined RNA and DNA from thousands of individual brain cells. This approach allowed them to pinpoint which cells behaved differently in people with depression and to identify genetic patterns that might explain those differences. The study included samples from 59 individuals diagnosed with depression and 41 without the condition.

Key Brain Cells Show Altered Activity

The analysis revealed changes in gene activity in two important types of brain cells. One was a group of excitatory neurons that play a role in regulating mood and responding to stress. The other was a subtype of microglia, immune cells in the brain that help control inflammation.

In both cell types, many genes showed different levels of activity in people with depression, suggesting that these systems may not be functioning normally. These disruptions could help explain how depression develops at a biological level.

Rethinking Depression as a Brain Disorder

By identifying the specific cells involved, the study strengthens the case that depression has a clear biological foundation. It also challenges outdated views that treat the condition as purely emotional or psychological.

"This research reinforces what neuroscience has been telling us for years," Turecki said. "Depression isn't just emotional, it reflects real, measurable changes in the brain."

What Comes Next for Depression Research

The researchers now plan to investigate how these cellular differences affect overall brain function. They also hope to determine whether therapies that target these cells could lead to more effective treatments in the future.

About the Study

The paper, titled "Single-nucleus chromatin accessibility profiling identifies cell types and functional variants contributing to major depression" by Anjali Chawla and Gustavo Turecki et al., was published in Nature Genetics.

Funding for the research was provided by the Canadian Institutes of Health Research, Brain Canada Foundation, Fonds de recherche du Québec -- Santé and the Healthy Brains, Healthy Lives initiative at McGill University.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough identifying specific brain cells involved in depression, a condition affecting millions globally. This discovery offers a clearer biological understanding of depression and opens pathways for targeted treatments, representing a meaningful advancement in mental health research with broad societal impact.
articles.personality_title: Scientists identify brain cells linked to depression for the first time
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Depression affects over 264 million people worldwide and has long been considered a complex condition involving emotions and biology. Until now, scientists had not clearly identified which brain cells are involved.

**What happened** – Researchers at McGill University and the Douglas Institute studied donated brain tissue from people with and without depression. Using advanced genetic tools, they found that two types of brain cells—excitatory neurons related to mood and stress, and microglia immune cells—show different gene activity in those with depression.

**Impact** – This discovery shows that depression is linked to specific biological changes in the brain, not just emotional states. It helps explain how depression develops and could lead to new treatments that target these exact brain cells.

**What's next step** – Scientists will now explore how these cell differences affect brain function and test if treatments aimed at these cells can better help people with depression.

**One-sentence takeaway** – For the first time, scientists have pinpointed specific brain cells whose altered activity is linked to depression, opening new paths for understanding and treating the condition.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques identifient pour la première fois les cellules cérébrales liées à la dépression
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La dépression touche plus de 264 millions de personnes dans le monde et est souvent vue comme une condition complexe mêlant émotions et biologie. Jusqu'à présent, les scientifiques n'avaient pas clairement identifié quelles cellules du cerveau étaient impliquées.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de l'Université McGill et de l'Institut Douglas ont étudié des tissus cérébraux donnés par des personnes avec et sans dépression. Grâce à des outils génétiques avancés, ils ont découvert que deux types de cellules cérébrales — des neurones excitateurs liés à l'humeur et au stress, et des microglies, cellules immunitaires — présentent une activité génétique différente chez les personnes dépressives.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre que la dépression est liée à des changements biologiques précis dans le cerveau, et pas seulement à des états émotionnels. Elle aide à comprendre comment la dépression se développe et pourrait mener à de nouveaux traitements ciblant ces cellules.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont maintenant étudier comment ces différences cellulaires affectent le fonctionnement du cerveau et tester si des traitements ciblant ces cellules peuvent mieux aider les personnes dépressives.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Pour la première fois, les scientifiques ont identifié des cellules cérébrales spécifiques dont l'activité modifiée est liée à la dépression, ouvrant de nouvelles voies pour comprendre et traiter cette maladie.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos identifican por primera vez las células cerebrales relacionadas con la depresión
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La depresión afecta a más de 264 millones de personas en todo el mundo y se ha considerado una condición compleja que involucra emociones y biología. Hasta ahora, los científicos no habían identificado claramente qué células del cerebro están involucradas.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad McGill y el Instituto Douglas estudiaron tejido cerebral donado por personas con y sin depresión. Usando herramientas genéticas avanzadas, encontraron que dos tipos de células cerebrales — neuronas excitatorias relacionadas con el estado de ánimo y el estrés, y microglías, células inmunitarias — muestran diferente actividad genética en personas con depresión.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento demuestra que la depresión está ligada a cambios biológicos específicos en el cerebro, no solo a estados emocionales. Ayuda a explicar cómo se desarrolla la depresión y podría conducir a nuevos tratamientos que apunten a estas células.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos ahora explorarán cómo estas diferencias celulares afectan la función cerebral y probarán si los tratamientos dirigidos a estas células pueden ayudar mejor a las personas con depresión.

**Resumen en una frase** – Por primera vez, los científicos han identificado células cerebrales específicas cuya actividad alterada está vinculada a la depresión, abriendo nuevos caminos para entender y tratar la enfermedad.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting showing two distinct, stylized brain cells—one as a glowing network of branching excitatory neurons radiating gentle light, and the other as a cluster of protective microglia shaped like softly glowing, shield-like forms—set against a calm, abstract background of intertwined DNA strands and subtle gene patterns in natural, muted colors.

articles.title: These 80-year-olds have the memory of 50-year-olds. Scientists now know why
articles.summary: A rare group of adults over 80, known as SuperAgers, are rewriting what we thought was possible for the aging brain. With memory abilities comparable to people decades younger, their brains either resist or withstand the damage typically linked to Alzheimer’s disease. Decades of research reveal that their social lifestyles and unique brain biology may hold the key to preserving cognitive function. Scientists believe these insights could pave the way for new strategies to delay or even prevent dementia.
articles.content:
For more than 25 years, researchers at Northwestern Medicine have been studying people age 80 and older known as "SuperAgers" to understand how some individuals maintain exceptional mental sharpness late in life.

These individuals consistently perform on memory tests at levels similar to people at least 30 years younger, challenging the long-standing belief that cognitive decline is unavoidable with age.

Over decades of research, scientists have noticed some lifestyle and personality traits that set SuperAgers apart from their peers, including being highly social and outgoing. Still, the most surprising discoveries have come from examining their brains. "It's really what we've found in their brains that's been so earth-shattering for us," said Dr. Sandra Weintraub, a professor of psychiatry and behavioral sciences and neurology at Northwestern University Feinberg School of Medicine.

By identifying both biological and behavioral patterns linked to SuperAging, researchers hope to develop new approaches to strengthen cognitive resilience and reduce the risk of Alzheimer's disease and other forms of dementia.

"Our findings show that exceptional memory in old age is not only possible but is linked to a distinct neurobiological profile. This opens the door to new interventions aimed at preserving brain health well into the later decades of life," said Weintraub, corresponding author of a new paper summarizing the results.

The findings were published as a perspective article in Alzheimer's & Dementia: The Journal of the Alzheimer's Association, part of a special issue marking the 40th anniversary of the National Institute on Aging's Alzheimer's Disease Centers Program and the 25th anniversary of the National Alzheimer Coordinating Center.

Resilient and Resistant Brains

The label "SuperAger" was introduced by Dr. M. Marsel Mesulam, who founded the Mesulam Center for Cognitive Neurology and Alzheimer's Disease at Northwestern in the late 1990s.

Since 2000, 290 participants have taken part in the program, and researchers have studied 77 donated SuperAger brains after death. Some of these brains showed the presence of amyloid and tau proteins (also known as plaques and tangles), which are strongly linked to Alzheimer's disease. Others showed no signs of these harmful proteins at all.

"What we realized is there are two mechanisms that lead someone to become a SuperAger," Weintraub said. "One is resistance: they don't make the plaques and tangles. Two is resilience: they make them, but they don't do anything to their brains."

Key Discoveries About SuperAgers

Researchers have uncovered several defining characteristics that help explain why SuperAgers maintain such strong cognitive abilities:

Exceptional memory performance: SuperAgers score at least 9 out of 15 on delayed word recall tests, matching the performance of people in their 50s and 60s.

SuperAgers score at least 9 out of 15 on delayed word recall tests, matching the performance of people in their 50s and 60s. Youthful brain structure: Unlike typical aging brains, they show little to no thinning of the cortex, the brain's outer layer. In some cases, a region called the anterior cingulate cortex is even thicker than in younger adults, supporting decision-making, emotion, and motivation.

Unlike typical aging brains, they show little to no thinning of the cortex, the brain's outer layer. In some cases, a region called the anterior cingulate cortex is even thicker than in younger adults, supporting decision-making, emotion, and motivation. Unique cellular features: SuperAgers have a higher number of von economo neurons, which are linked to social behavior, along with larger entorhinal neurons that play a critical role in memory.

SuperAgers have a higher number of von economo neurons, which are linked to social behavior, along with larger entorhinal neurons that play a critical role in memory. Strong social connections: While their exercise habits and lifestyles vary, most SuperAgers are highly social and maintain close relationships.

Brain Donation and Long-Term Discovery

At the Mesulam Center, participants are evaluated each year and can choose to donate their brains for scientific study after death. These donations have been essential to many of the program's most important findings.

"Many of the findings from this paper stem from the examination of brain specimens of generous, dedicated SuperAgers who were followed for decades," said co-author Dr. Tamar Gefen, associate professor of psychiatry and behavioral sciences at Feinberg, director of Feinberg's Laboratory for Translational Neuropsychology and a neuropsychologist at the Mesulam Center. "I am constantly amazed by how brain donation can enable discovery long after death, offering a kind of scientific immortality."

A Landmark Study on SuperAging

The research is detailed in a perspective article titled "The First 25 Years of the Northwestern SuperAging Program." Additional contributors include Dr. Mesulam and Changiz Geula, a research professor of cell and developmental biology and neuroscience at Feinberg and a member of the Mesulam Center.

Researchers hope these findings will guide future strategies to protect brain health and help more people maintain sharp thinking well into old age.

Key Points
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in understanding how some elderly individuals maintain exceptional cognitive function, challenging assumptions about aging and dementia. The research has broad implications for developing new interventions to prevent or delay Alzheimer's disease, benefiting society at large. The article provides detailed context about the study, its findings, and its potential impact on brain health.
articles.personality_title: Scientists uncover why some 80-year-olds have the memory of 50-year-olds
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long believed that memory and thinking skills naturally get worse as people grow very old. But a special group of adults over 80, called SuperAgers, have memory abilities similar to people 30 years younger. Researchers at Northwestern Medicine have been studying these SuperAgers for more than 25 years to understand why.

**What happened** – The scientists tested memory and studied the brains of 290 SuperAgers, including 77 brains donated after death. They found that SuperAgers either avoid or resist the brain damage usually linked to Alzheimer’s disease. Some do not develop harmful plaques and tangles in their brains, while others have these but their brains seem to handle them without losing memory. SuperAgers also tend to be very social and have unique brain features, like thicker brain areas important for decision-making and more special neurons linked to social skills and memory.

**Impact** – These discoveries are important because they show that having a sharp memory in old age is possible and linked to how the brain is built and how people live. Understanding SuperAgers’ brains could help scientists create new ways to keep more people’s brains healthy and delay or prevent diseases like Alzheimer’s.

**What’s next step** – Researchers plan to use what they learned from SuperAgers to develop new treatments and lifestyle advice that might help others protect their memory as they grow older. Continued study of donated brains will also help uncover more details about how some brains stay strong.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Studying SuperAgers reveals that some people’s brains resist or handle damage from aging differently, offering clues to preserving memory well into old age.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent pourquoi certains octogénaires ont la mémoire d’un quinquagénaire
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – On pensait souvent que la mémoire et les capacités intellectuelles déclinaient naturellement avec l’âge avancé. Pourtant, un groupe spécial de personnes de plus de 80 ans, appelées SuperAgers, ont une mémoire comparable à celle de personnes de 30 ans de moins. Des chercheurs de Northwestern Medicine étudient ces SuperAgers depuis plus de 25 ans pour comprendre pourquoi.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Les scientifiques ont testé la mémoire et examiné le cerveau de 290 SuperAgers, dont 77 cerveaux donnés après leur décès. Ils ont découvert que ces personnes évitent ou résistent aux dommages cérébraux liés à la maladie d’Alzheimer. Certains ne développent pas les plaques et enchevêtrements nocifs dans leur cerveau, tandis que d’autres les ont mais leur cerveau gère ces dommages sans perte de mémoire. Les SuperAgers sont aussi très sociaux et ont des particularités cérébrales, comme des zones plus épaisses du cerveau liées à la prise de décision et plus de neurones spéciaux associés aux compétences sociales et à la mémoire.

**Impact** – Ces découvertes sont importantes car elles montrent qu’il est possible d’avoir une mémoire excellente à un âge avancé, liée à la structure du cerveau et au mode de vie. Comprendre les SuperAgers pourrait aider à créer de nouvelles façons de garder le cerveau en bonne santé et de retarder ou prévenir des maladies comme Alzheimer.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent utiliser les enseignements tirés des SuperAgers pour développer des traitements et conseils de vie qui aideraient d’autres personnes à protéger leur mémoire en vieillissant. L’étude continue des cerveaux donnés aidera aussi à découvrir davantage comment certains cerveaux restent forts.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Étudier les SuperAgers montre que certains cerveaux résistent ou gèrent différemment les dommages liés à l’âge, donnant des pistes pour préserver la mémoire longtemps.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren por qué algunos mayores de 80 años tienen memoria de personas de 50
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante mucho tiempo se pensó que la memoria y las habilidades mentales empeoran naturalmente en la vejez. Pero un grupo especial de personas mayores de 80 años, llamados SuperAgers, tienen una memoria similar a la de personas 30 años más jóvenes. Investigadores de Northwestern Medicine han estudiado a estos SuperAgers por más de 25 años para entender por qué.

**Qué pasó** – Los científicos evaluaron la memoria y examinaron los cerebros de 290 SuperAgers, incluyendo 77 cerebros donados después de su muerte. Descubrieron que los SuperAgers evitan o resisten el daño cerebral relacionado con el Alzheimer. Algunos no desarrollan las placas y ovillos dañinos en el cerebro, mientras que otros sí, pero sus cerebros manejan ese daño sin perder memoria. Los SuperAgers también suelen ser muy sociales y tienen características cerebrales únicas, como áreas más gruesas en el cerebro importantes para tomar decisiones y más neuronas especiales relacionadas con el comportamiento social y la memoria.

**Impacto** – Estos hallazgos son importantes porque muestran que tener una memoria fuerte en la vejez es posible y está relacionado con la biología del cerebro y el estilo de vida. Entender a los SuperAgers podría ayudar a crear nuevas formas de mantener el cerebro saludable y retrasar o prevenir enfermedades como el Alzheimer.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean usar lo aprendido de los SuperAgers para desarrollar nuevos tratamientos y consejos de vida que ayuden a otras personas a proteger su memoria al envejecer. El estudio continuo de cerebros donados también ayudará a descubrir más detalles sobre cómo algunos cerebros se mantienen fuertes.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Estudiar a los SuperAgers muestra que algunos cerebros resisten o manejan el daño del envejecimiento de manera diferente, ofreciendo pistas para preservar la memoria durante más tiempo.
articles.image_prompt: An intricate, warmly colored painting of a vibrant, flourishing brain shaped like an ancient oak tree with thick, sturdy branches symbolizing youthful cortical thickness, intertwined with glowing, distinctive von Economo neuron-like structures as golden threads, surrounded by a gentle circle of interconnected, abstract human silhouettes representing strong social bonds and enduring cognitive resilience.

articles.title: Simple “gut reset” may stop weight gain after Ozempic or Wegovy
articles.summary: A new minimally invasive procedure may help people keep weight off after stopping popular drugs like Ozempic and semaglutide—something most patients struggle with. In a clinical trial, those who underwent a technique called duodenal mucosal resurfacing regained far less weight compared to others after discontinuing the medication. The procedure works by renewing the lining of the upper small intestine, potentially “resetting” metabolism and preserving the benefits of weight loss.
articles.content:
A minimally invasive outpatient procedure may help people avoid regaining weight after stopping popular medications like Ozempic and semaglutide, according to research being presented at Digestive Disease Week® (DDW) 2026. About 70% of people who stop these drugs eventually regain much of the weight they lost, often within 18 months. Nearly one in five adults with obesity has used a glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1), highlighting the scale of this challenge.

Researchers report the first blinded, randomized, sham-controlled evidence that a procedure called duodenal mucosal resurfacing may offer a safe and lasting way to maintain weight loss without ongoing medication. The findings suggest it could help patients hold onto the benefits they achieved while taking drugs such as Ozempic or other GLP-1 therapies.

"As effective as GLP-1 medications are, many people stop taking them because of cost, side effects or simply not wanting to take a drug long-term," said lead author Shelby Sullivan, MD, director of the Endoscopic Bariatric and Metabolic Program at Dartmouth Health Weight Center and professor of medicine, Dartmouth Geisel School of Medicine. "But, if they stop these medications, weight regain occurs in the vast majority of patients, and the metabolic benefits are lost. Finding a treatment that allows patients to stop these medications without weight regain or loss of metabolic benefit is a huge unmet need. These findings indicate that this minimally invasive procedure may provide lasting weight-loss maintenance."

How the "Gut Reset" Procedure Works

Duodenal mucosal resurfacing is an investigational endoscopic treatment that uses controlled heat to remove damaged tissue from the inner lining of the duodenum, the first section of the small intestine just below the stomach. This process, which ablates (burns) the unhealthy mucosal layer, encourages the growth of new, healthier tissue.

The ongoing REMAIN-1 trial is designed to test whether this renewal of the intestinal lining can trigger a lasting metabolic reset, helping the body maintain weight loss after stopping medications like semaglutide or tirzepatide.

Trial Results Show Less Weight Regain

The current findings come from an early group of participants with six months of follow-up data. Among 45 people in this cohort, 29 received the resurfacing treatment while 16 underwent a sham procedure. All participants had previously lost at least 15% of their body weight using tirzepatide before stopping the drug.

On average, patients lost about 40 pounds while on GLP-1 therapy. Six months after discontinuing the medication, those in the control group regained significantly more weight. Participants who received the sham procedure regained about 40% more weight than those who underwent the actual treatment.

In addition, patients who had more extensive resurfacing regained only about 7 pounds and kept more than 80% of their weight loss. By comparison, the control group regained roughly twice as much. The gap between the two groups continued to widen from one to six months after the procedure, suggesting the benefits may persist and even strengthen over time.

"What's particularly encouraging is that the benefit appears to increase over time rather than fade, and that it behaves like a drug in terms of dose response," Dr. Sullivan said. "That gives us confidence that we're targeting the right biology."

Safety and Recovery

No serious complications were reported from either the device or the procedure. Recovery is relatively quick, with most patients returning to normal activities within about a day.

"Other than recovering from the general anesthesia, there isn't much recovery time involved," Dr. Sullivan said. "You can be back to your daily routine in about a day. Participants could not tell if they had the sham or real procedure because there are not a lot of symptoms after the procedure."

Why the Gut Is Key to Weight Regulation

The treatment targets the small intestine, where many of the hormones affected by GLP-1 drugs are produced. Over time, diets high in fat and sugar can alter the lining of the duodenum, changing how the body processes food and regulates hormones. These changes can contribute to insulin resistance and metabolic disease.

By restoring a healthier mucosal layer, the procedure aims to reset how the body responds to food, helping stabilize metabolism at a lower body weight after stopping medications like Ozempic.

What Comes Next

Duodenal mucosal resurfacing is still considered investigational. The larger REMAIN-1 study includes more than 300 participants and is fully enrolled and randomized. Researchers expect topline six-month data from the pivotal cohort in early fourth quarter of 2026, followed by a planned marketing submission later that year.

Dr. Sullivan will present the findings from the study, "Duodenal mucosal resurfacing prevents weight regain after tirzepatide withdrawal: REMAIN-1 multicenter, randomized, double-blind, sham-controlled clinical trial -- midpoint cohort results," abstract 642, at 8:30 a.m. CDT, Monday, May 4.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a new minimally invasive medical procedure that helps maintain weight loss after stopping widely used obesity drugs, addressing a major unmet need with broad public health implications. The clinical trial evidence is detailed and shows significant, lasting benefits with a safe and quick recovery, indicating a meaningful breakthrough in obesity treatment.
articles.personality_title: New Gut Procedure Helps Keep Weight Off After Stopping Ozempic or Wegovy
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many people use medications like Ozempic or Wegovy to lose weight, but most regain the weight once they stop taking these drugs. About 70% of patients face this problem within 18 months. These drugs work by changing hormones in the gut to control appetite and blood sugar.

**What happened** – Researchers tested a new, simple procedure called duodenal mucosal resurfacing (DMR) that treats the upper small intestine by removing damaged tissue so healthier tissue can grow. In a clinical trial, 29 patients who had lost weight on drugs like tirzepatide underwent this procedure after stopping medication. They regained much less weight compared to 16 patients who had a fake (sham) procedure.

**Impact** – The procedure helped patients keep more than 80% of their weight loss six months after stopping medication. Those who did not get the real treatment regained almost twice as much weight. The benefits even grew stronger over time. The treatment is safe, with quick recovery and no serious side effects, offering a new way to maintain weight loss without ongoing drugs.

**What’s next step** – The larger REMAIN-1 study with over 300 participants is ongoing, with full results expected by late 2026. If confirmed, this procedure could become a new option for people to keep weight off after using weight-loss drugs.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new gut lining treatment shows promise in helping people maintain weight loss after stopping popular obesity medications like Ozempic or Wegovy.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle procédure intestinale aide à garder du poids après l'arrêt d'Ozempic ou Wegovy
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Beaucoup de personnes utilisent des médicaments comme Ozempic ou Wegovy pour perdre du poids, mais la majorité reprend les kilos une fois qu'elles arrêtent ces traitements. Environ 70 % des patients rencontrent ce problème dans les 18 mois. Ces médicaments agissent en modifiant les hormones de l'intestin pour contrôler l'appétit et le sucre dans le sang.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs ont testé une nouvelle procédure simple appelée resurfaçage de la muqueuse duodénale (RMD) qui traite la partie supérieure de l'intestin grêle en enlevant les tissus abîmés pour permettre la croissance de tissus plus sains. Lors d’un essai clinique, 29 patients ayant perdu du poids avec des médicaments comme le tirzépatide ont subi cette procédure après l'arrêt du traitement. Ils ont repris beaucoup moins de poids comparé à 16 patients ayant eu une fausse procédure.

**Impact** – La procédure a aidé les patients à conserver plus de 80 % de leur perte de poids six mois après l'arrêt du médicament. Ceux qui n'ont pas reçu le traitement réel ont repris presque deux fois plus de poids. Les bénéfices ont même augmenté avec le temps. Le traitement est sûr, avec une récupération rapide et sans effets secondaires graves, offrant une nouvelle façon de maintenir la perte de poids sans médicaments continus.

**Prochaine étape** – L’étude plus large REMAIN-1, avec plus de 300 participants, est en cours, avec des résultats complets attendus fin 2026. Si les résultats sont confirmés, cette procédure pourrait devenir une nouvelle option pour aider les personnes à garder leur poids après un traitement médicamenteux.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Un nouveau traitement de la muqueuse intestinale montre qu’il peut aider à maintenir la perte de poids après l’arrêt de médicaments populaires comme Ozempic ou Wegovy.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo procedimiento intestinal ayuda a mantener el peso tras dejar Ozempic o Wegovy
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchas personas usan medicamentos como Ozempic o Wegovy para bajar de peso, pero la mayoría recupera el peso al dejar de tomarlos. Cerca del 70 % de los pacientes enfrentan este problema dentro de 18 meses. Estos medicamentos funcionan al cambiar las hormonas del intestino que controlan el apetito y el azúcar en la sangre.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores probaron un nuevo procedimiento sencillo llamado remodelación mucosal duodenal (RMD) que trata la parte superior del intestino delgado al eliminar tejido dañado para que crezca tejido más saludable. En un ensayo clínico, 29 pacientes que habían perdido peso con medicamentos como tirzepatida se sometieron a este procedimiento tras dejar la medicación. Recuperaron mucho menos peso en comparación con 16 pacientes que recibieron un procedimiento falso.

**Impacto** – El procedimiento ayudó a los pacientes a conservar más del 80 % de su pérdida de peso seis meses después de dejar la medicación. Los que no recibieron el tratamiento real recuperaron casi el doble de peso. Los beneficios incluso aumentaron con el tiempo. El tratamiento es seguro, con recuperación rápida y sin efectos secundarios graves, ofreciendo una nueva forma de mantener el peso sin medicamentos continuos.

**Próximo paso** – El estudio más grande REMAIN-1, con más de 300 participantes, está en marcha y se esperan resultados completos a finales de 2026. Si se confirman, este procedimiento podría ser una nueva opción para que las personas mantengan su peso tras usar medicamentos para bajar de peso.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Un nuevo tratamiento del revestimiento intestinal muestra que puede ayudar a mantener la pérdida de peso después de dejar medicamentos populares como Ozempic o Wegovy.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a stylized small intestine section gently glowing with renewed, smooth inner lining textures bathed in soft natural hues, alongside a delicate, abstract representation of a healing flame symbolizing controlled heat treatment, set against a calm, neutral background to evoke health, renewal, and lasting metabolic balance.

articles.title: Scientists create plastic that destroys viruses on contact
articles.summary: A new virus-fighting plastic film could transform everyday surfaces into invisible defenders against disease. Instead of relying on chemicals, this flexible material is covered in microscopic pillars that physically stretch viruses until they burst, rendering them harmless. In lab tests, it destroyed or disabled about 94% of virus particles within an hour, showing impressive effectiveness.
articles.content:
Scientists have created a thin plastic film that can physically destroy viruses the moment they land on its surface. The breakthrough could help reduce the spread of disease from frequently touched items like smartphones, keyboards, and hospital equipment.

Beyond its effectiveness, the material is also designed to be practical for real-world use. Unlike earlier antiviral surfaces made from metals or silicon, this new approach uses flexible plastic that can be produced at scale.

How Nanopillars Tear Viruses Apart

The film is made from acrylic and covered with extremely small structures known as nanopillars. These tiny features grip onto a virus and stretch its outer layer until it breaks apart. Instead of relying on chemical disinfectants, the surface uses mechanical force to disable the virus.

Research published in Advanced Science found that this stretching method is more effective than earlier designs that attempted to puncture viruses.

Lab Tests Show Strong Virus Inactivation

In experiments using the human parainfluenza virus 3 (hPIV-3) -- which causes bronchiolitis and pneumonia -- the results were striking. Within one hour of contact, about 94% of virus particles were either torn apart or damaged so severely that they could no longer reproduce and cause infection.

Study lead author and PhD candidate Samson Mah from Australia's RMIT University said the team intentionally used low-cost materials that could be manufactured easily.

"As nanofabrication tools get better, our results give a clearer guide to which nanopatterns work best to kill viruses," he said.

"We could one day have surfaces like phone screens, keyboards and hospital tables covered with this film, killing viruses on contact without using harsh chemicals.

"Our mold can be adapted to roll-to-roll manufacturing, meaning antiviral plastic films could be produced at scale with existing factory equipment."

Why Nanopillar Spacing Matters Most

The researchers also discovered that how closely the nanopillars are spaced plays a much bigger role than how tall they are.

"By tweaking the spacing and height of the nanopillars, we discovered how tightly they are packed together is far more important than how tall they are for breaking viruses apart," Mah said.

"When the nanopillars are closer together, more of them can press on the same virus at once, stretching its outer shell past breaking point."

A Simple Design Rule for Virus-Killing Surfaces

Earlier work on rigid materials like nanospike silicon showed that viruses could be physically disrupted. This study expands on that idea by showing that both sharp and blunt nanoscale features can be effective when arranged correctly.

The findings suggest a clear design principle: the closer the nanostructures such as spikes or nanopillars are to each other, the more effective they are at destroying viruses.

The strongest performance came from surfaces where nanopillars were spaced about 60 nanometers apart. Increasing that distance to 100 nanometers reduced the antiviral effect, while spacing of 200 nanometers nearly eliminated it.

Next Steps and Real-World Potential

So far, the research has focused on hPIV-3, which is an enveloped virus with a fatty outer membrane. The team now plans to test smaller and non-enveloped viruses to determine how broadly the technology can be applied.

An enveloped virus has a fragile fatty membrane around it that can be more easily disrupted by nanopillars, while a non-enveloped virus lacks this outer layer, making it harder to kill.

Scientists also want to examine how well the textured film works on curved surfaces, since curvature can change the spacing between nanopillars.

Study co-author Distinguished Professor Elena Ivanova from RMIT said the team is eager to move toward real-world applications.

"We think this texturing is a strong candidate for everyday use and we're ready to partner with companies to refine it for large-scale manufacturing," she said.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant scientific breakthrough in creating a plastic film that physically destroys viruses on contact, which could reduce disease spread on everyday surfaces. The technology uses scalable, low-cost materials and has broad potential applications in public health, especially in high-touch environments like hospitals and personal devices. The detailed explanation of the mechanism and lab test results adds substance and credibility to the story.
articles.personality_title: New plastic film destroys viruses by stretching them apart
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists are looking for ways to stop viruses from spreading through surfaces we touch every day, like phones and keyboards. Traditional methods often use chemicals or metals, but these can be hard to produce or use widely.

**What happened** – Researchers at RMIT University in Australia created a thin, flexible plastic film covered with tiny pillars called nanopillars. When a virus lands on this film, the nanopillars grab and stretch the virus’s outer layer until it breaks, destroying the virus. Lab tests showed that within one hour, the film disabled about 94% of a virus that causes lung infections.

**Impact** – This plastic film uses mechanical force, not chemicals, to kill viruses. It can be made cheaply and in large amounts using existing factory machines. The discovery that the spacing between nanopillars matters most helps guide how to design better virus-killing surfaces. This could mean safer phones, hospital equipment, and other common items.

**What's next step** – The team will test the film on different types of viruses, including ones without a fatty outer layer, which are harder to destroy. They also want to see how the film works on curved surfaces like phone screens. The researchers are ready to work with companies to produce the film on a large scale.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new low-cost plastic film uses tiny pillars to physically break viruses on contact, offering a promising way to reduce virus spread on everyday surfaces.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau film plastique détruit les virus en les étirant
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques cherchent des moyens d'arrêter la propagation des virus sur les surfaces que nous touchons tous les jours, comme les téléphones et les claviers. Les méthodes traditionnelles utilisent souvent des produits chimiques ou des métaux, mais ceux-ci peuvent être difficiles à produire ou à utiliser à grande échelle.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de l'université RMIT en Australie ont créé un film plastique fin et flexible recouvert de minuscules piliers appelés nanopiliers. Lorsqu'un virus touche ce film, les nanopiliers attrapent et étirent la couche extérieure du virus jusqu'à ce qu'elle se rompe, détruisant ainsi le virus. Des tests en laboratoire ont montré que le film désactivait environ 94 % d'un virus responsable d'infections pulmonaires en une heure.

**Impact** – Ce film plastique utilise une force mécanique, pas des produits chimiques, pour tuer les virus. Il peut être fabriqué à faible coût et en grande quantité avec des machines déjà existantes. La découverte que l'espacement entre les nanopiliers est le facteur le plus important aide à concevoir de meilleures surfaces antivirales. Cela pourrait rendre les téléphones, le matériel hospitalier et d'autres objets courants plus sûrs.

**Prochaine étape** – L'équipe testera le film sur différents types de virus, y compris ceux sans couche grasse extérieure, qui sont plus difficiles à détruire. Ils veulent aussi vérifier comment le film fonctionne sur des surfaces courbes comme les écrans de téléphone. Les chercheurs sont prêts à collaborer avec des entreprises pour produire le film à grande échelle.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un nouveau film plastique peu coûteux utilise de minuscules piliers pour briser physiquement les virus au contact, offrant un moyen prometteur de réduire leur propagation sur les surfaces du quotidien.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo film plástico destruye virus estirándolos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos buscan formas de detener la propagación de virus en superficies que tocamos a diario, como teléfonos y teclados. Los métodos tradicionales suelen usar químicos o metales, pero estos pueden ser difíciles de producir o usar a gran escala.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad RMIT en Australia crearon una película plástica delgada y flexible cubierta con pequeños pilares llamados nanopilares. Cuando un virus cae sobre esta película, los nanopilares agarran y estiran la capa externa del virus hasta romperla, destruyendo el virus. En pruebas de laboratorio, la película inactivó alrededor del 94 % de un virus que causa infecciones pulmonares en una hora.

**Impacto** – Esta película plástica usa fuerza mecánica, no químicos, para matar virus. Puede fabricarse a bajo costo y en grandes cantidades con máquinas ya existentes. El descubrimiento de que el espacio entre nanopilares es lo más importante ayuda a diseñar mejores superficies antivirales. Esto podría hacer más seguros los teléfonos, equipos hospitalarios y otros objetos comunes.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo probará la película con diferentes tipos de virus, incluidos aquellos sin capa grasa externa, que son más difíciles de destruir. También quieren ver cómo funciona la película en superficies curvas como pantallas de teléfonos. Los investigadores están listos para colaborar con empresas para producir la película a gran escala.

**Resumen en una frase** – Una nueva película plástica económica usa pequeños pilares para romper físicamente virus al contacto, ofreciendo una forma prometedora de reducir la propagación viral en superficies diarias.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a flexible translucent plastic film magnified to reveal dense, evenly spaced nanopillars gently stretching and breaking apart delicate, stylized virus shapes on contact, set against a soft, neutral background that evokes cleanliness and innovation.
